soap:
SOAP · lance-format/lance#6365 — feat: compile-time feature-gated Rust backtrace for JNI errors

Subjective
  First-time contributor (summaryzb) proposes a compile-time feature-gated Rust backtrace surfaced through the JNI error boundary into Java exception messages. Two-tier switch: (1) #[cfg(feature="backtrace")] swaps lance-core's implicit backtrace: MaybeBacktrace snafu field between a real Option<Backtrace> struct and a zero-sized unit struct, so default builds pay no cost; (2) even with the feature compiled in, capture is gated at runtime by RUST_BACKTRACE via std::backtrace::Backtrace. lance-jni's From<LanceError> for Error appends a "Rust backtrace:" suffix. PR body is candid about scope. Good tests on both sides. CI green, but GitHub reports mergeable_state: dirty — real conflicts against main.

Objective
  - rust/lance-core/src/error.rs: adds a backtrace_support module defined twice under complementary cfgs (satisfies snafu GenerateImplicitData/AsBacktrace, available in snafu 0.9). Adds #[snafu(implicit)] backtrace: MaybeBacktrace to struct-style variants, plus Error::backtrace() with two cfg-gated impls: feature-off returns None unconditionally; feature-on has an explicit, non-wildcard match over every named variant (intentional, so a new variant triggers a compile error if a backtrace field is forgotten).
  - THIS EXHAUSTIVENESS GUARANTEE IS ALREADY VIOLATED. Current main's error.rs has two variants absent from this diff: Timeout {message, location} and DiskCapExceeded {cap_bytes, used_bytes, location}. Neither appears in the PR's feature-on match. With no _ wildcard, cargo build --features backtrace against main WILL NOT COMPILE (non-exhaustive match). mergeable_state: dirty + 3-month-old branch (opened 2026-03-31) confirm divergence. The Cargo.lock diff pulling in unrelated io-uring/libc/moka deps corroborates the branch predates workspace history and needs a real rebase.
  - java/lance-jni/src/error.rs: From<LanceError> computes backtrace_suffix unconditionally, appends to every mapped message. Retains _ => runtime_error wildcard, so not exposed to the exhaustiveness trap.
  - Tests: 9 new JNI unit tests (exception-class mapping + no-suffix-when-off); 3 new lance-core tests. test_backtrace_captured_when_feature_enabled honestly documents the RUST_BACKTRACE env-caching caveat.
  - java/pom.xml: adds <rust.features> Maven property (default empty) threaded into cargo build/test — clean, backwards-compatible opt-in.
  - Feature wiring across lance/lance-core/lance-jni Cargo.toml is consistent.
  - Could not read: nothing (7/7 files, no truncation).

Assessment
  risk: HIGH (up from packet's med) — the PR's own stated safety invariant (exhaustive match => compiler-enforced coverage) is already violated by variants merged into main after the branch was cut. Guaranteed compile failure on rebase, under the exact feature flag the PR adds. Pure error-plumbing; no IO/concurrency surface. Blocking issue is staleness, not architecture — the design itself is sound and the zero-cost-when-disabled claim holds structurally.
  urgency: high (first-time contributor, core crate) · escalation: none forced · could not read: none

Plan
  1. Rebase onto main; add #[snafu(implicit)] backtrace: MaybeBacktrace to Timeout and DiskCapExceeded (and any other new variant), add both to the backtrace() match arm.
  2. Confirm cargo build/test -p lance-core --features backtrace succeed post-rebase — the real acceptance criterion (CI ran against the stale base and wouldn't catch this).
  3. Non-blocking: consider whether the no-wildcard match is worth the recurring rebase-conflict tax vs a wildcard + debug_assert — author's tradeoff is defensible.

RECOMMENDATION: block
  confidence: high — exhaustiveness/compile-break finding independently verified against live main.
summary: Design is sound and well-tested, but the PR is 3 months stale against main: mergeable_state is 'dirty' with real conflicts, and main has since added Error::Timeout and Error::DiskCapExceeded variants absent from the PR's intentionally-exhaustive (no wildcard) match in Error::backtrace(). Once rebased, cargo build --features backtrace will fail to compile with a non-exhaustive-match error under lance-core's own feature flag.

comments.label: issue
comments.body:
issue(blocking): This match is intentionally exhaustive with no wildcard arm (per the comment at line 538) so new variants force a compiler error here. That guarantee is already violated: current main has Error::Timeout { message, location } and Error::DiskCapExceeded { cap_bytes, used_bytes, location }, neither of which exists in this diff nor in this match. After rebasing onto main (mergeable_state is currently 'dirty'), cargo build -p lance-core --features backtrace will fail with a non-exhaustive-match compile error.

Acceptance: add #[snafu(implicit)] backtrace: MaybeBacktrace to Timeout and DiskCapExceeded (and any variant added since 2026-03-31), add both to this match's backtrace-bearing arm, and confirm cargo build -p lance-core --features backtrace && cargo test -p lance-core --features backtrace succeed post-rebase.

comments.label: nit
comments.body: nit: this #[cfg(not(feature = "backtrace"))] arm unconditionally returns None regardless of variant count, so it isn't affected by the exhaustiveness issue above — only the feature-enabled match (line 509) needs the new variants added.

comments.label: praise
comments.body: praise: appreciate that test_backtrace_captured_when_feature_enabled's comment is upfront about the RUST_BACKTRACE env-caching limitation instead of presenting the test as stronger coverage than it is.

comments.label: question
comments.body: question: this lockfile diff pulls in io-uring, libc, and moka as new transitive deps of lance-jni — none obviously related to backtraces. Is this purely lockfile drift from rebasing a stale branch (will resolve on rebase), or does something in this PR actually pull these in? Worth a sanity check that cargo update isn't silently over-bumping unrelated deps.

comments.label: praise
comments.body: praise: threading -Drust.features through as an empty-default Maven property is a clean, backwards-compatible way to make the feature opt-in at build time without touching the default path.
